content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 20, 2007

Robert Gaumer
Chief Counsel
Alameda County Employees' Retirement Assoc.
475 14th Street, Suite 1000
Oakland, California 94612

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-07-076

Dear Mr. Gaumer:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding certain financial
interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Also, please note
that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no
opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law
conflicts of interest or Government Code Section 1090. The Commission does not act as
a finder of fact when providing assistance; this assistance is based solely on the facts you
provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, we are providing you with
general advice and are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

QUESTION

Are employees of the various investment management firms that contract with the
Alameda County Employees' Retirement Association ("ACERA") included within the
definition of "consultant" under the Act such that they are required to file Statements of
Economic Interests ("SEI" or "Form 700")?

_______________
¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All references to code "sections" shall be to the
Government Code, and all references to "regulations" shall be to title 2 of the state's Code of Regulations,
unless otherwise noted.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Reg. 18329(c).)

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

It appears that at least some employees of investment managers hired by ACERA,
possess a level of discretion and influence over investment decisions regarding ACERA's
funds that would make them subject to economic disclosure provisions under the Act.
But without: (1) information regarding the specific terms of any contracts between
ACERA and any of its one or more investment managers, or (2) information regarding
the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in duties similar to those
being performed by employees of the investment managers described above, we cannot
definitively determine whether or which investment manager employees would be
"consultants" under the Act, and obligated to file Statements of Economic Interests.

FACTS

The Alameda County Employees' Retirement Association ("ACERA") is an
independent public pension fund established under the authority of the County
Employees Retirement Law of 1937 (Sections 31450 et seq.). ACERA's Board of
Retirement ("Board") is comprised of nine members and two alternates.

The Board is charged with the responsibility of managing the retirement system,
including investment of the retirement fund. The Board has the sole authority to develop
and execute the investment policy. The investments are based on a comprehensive
financial plan that takes into consideration various investment mixes, their resulting risk
and return levels, and the current and projected liabilities of the plan.

ACERA has a Chief Investment Officer ("CIO") whose duties include, but are not
limited to, overseeing the retirement fund's investment program activities, implementing
the Board/ Investment Committee decisions, making recommendations to the Investment
Committee regarding management of the retirement fund, and recommending
investment-related policies and procedures to the Investment Committee. The CIO
monitors the performance and compliance of ACERA's investment managers with all
applicable laws, Board-established policies, guidelines and procedures, as well as the
Specific Investment Guidelines ("Specific Guidelines") set out in the investment
agreement between ACERA and each investment manager.

The ACERA investment managers (that are outside contractors and not
employees of ACERA) have full discretion to direct the purchase, exchange, sale and
reinvestment of the assets of their accounts. Investment managers place orders to buy
and sell securities. The discretion of each individual investment manager is governed by
the ACERA General Investment Guidelines, Policies and Procedures ("General
Guidelines") for the Managed Accounts as well as the Specific Guidelines for each
investment manager.

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 3

The Board monitors the performance of managers to: (1) evaluate how well they
achieve their investment objectives; (2) ensure that they adhere to their established
investment approach/style and do not attempt to index returns to preserve stellar
performance or take extraordinary risk to overcome poor performance; (3) identify issues
or trends that have the potential to result in losses to the retirement fund; and
(4) acknowledge when investment managers are not performing to expectations so the
appropriate remedial action can be taken. This monitoring procedure is described in
greater detail on pages 11-13 of the General Guidelines.

Each investment manager is responsible for investing a portion of the assets of
ACERA according to an asset allocation approved by the Board. The different asset
classes are U.S. Equity, International Equity, Fixed Income, and Real Estate.

No facts are provided regarding the specific terms of any contracts between
ACERA and any of its one or more investment managers, and no facts are provided
regarding the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in duties similar
to those being performed by employees of the investment managers described above.

ANALYSIS

Under the Act, state and local "public officials" file periodic statements of
economic interests (Form 700) for the purpose of disclosing pertinent economic interests.
(See Sections 87200 et seq.; 87300 et seq.; Section 82048 [defining "public official"].)
These individuals are required to file the Form 700 pursuant to either Section 87200 or
Section 87300.

We will begin our analysis with Section 87200, which imposes broad disclosure
obligations on various high level public officials, including "other public officials who
manage public investments." (Section 87200.) We will also analyze whether the people
are "consultants" pursuant to Section 87300. (Sections 82019(a)
& 87300.)

A. "Other Public Officials Who Manage Public Investments" Under Section 87200
and Regulation 18701(b)(1).

Regulation 18701(b)(1) defines "other public officials who manage public
investments" to include:

"(A) Members of boards and commissions, including pension
and retirement boards or commissions, or of committees thereof, who
exercise responsibility for the management of public investments;

"(B) High-level officers and employees of public agencies who
exercise primary responsibility for the management of public
investments, such as chief or principal investment officers or chief

File No. 1-07-076
Page No. 4

financial managers. This category shall not include officers and
employees who work under the supervision of the chief or principal
investment officers or the chief financial managers; and

"(C) Individuals who, pursuant to a contract with a state or local
government agency, perform the same or substantially all the same
functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials
described in subdivision (b)(1)(B) above." (Ibid.)

Regulation 18701(b) (2), (3) and (4) set forth the following definitions:

"(2) 'Public investments' means the investment of public moneys
in real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the production of
revenue or other financial return.

"(3) 'Public moneys' means all moneys belonging to, received
by, or held by, the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public
agency therein, or by an officer thereof acting in his or her official
capacity, and includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidences of
indebtedness, trust funds held by public pension and retirement systems,
deferred compensation funds held for investments by public agencies,
and public moneys held by a financial institution under a trust indenture
to which a public agency is a party.

"(4) 'Management of public investments' means the following
non-ministerial functions: directing the investment of public moneys;
formulating or approving investment policies; approving or establishing
guidelines for asset allocations; or approving investment transactions."
(Ibid.)

We now analyze whether employees of the various investment management firms
that contract with ACERA are "other public officials who manage public investments"
under Regulation 18701(b)(1). Initially, we observe that what these employees do
(i.e., "direct the purchase, exchange, sale and reinvestment of the assets of their
accounts . . . [and] place orders to buy and sell securities") fits the definition of
"management of public investments" in Regulation 18701(b)(4).

Since these employees are employed by private investment management firms,
they are neither: (A) "[m]embers of boards and commissions" (or committees thereof)
that exercise responsibility for the management of public investments, or (B) "[h]igh-
level officers and employees of public agencies who exercise primary responsibility for
the management of public investments, such as chief or principal investment officers or
chief financial managers." (See Reg. 18701(b)(1)(A)&(B).)

File No. 1-07-076
Page No. 5

In order to fit within the last category of "other public officials who manage
public investments" these private employees must be "[i]ndividuals who, pursuant to a
contract with a state or local government agency, perform the same or substantially all
the same functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in
subsection (b)(1)(B) above." (Reg. 18701(b)(1)(C).)

Subsection (b)(1)(B) explicitly states "[t]his category shall not include officers
and employees who finance under the supervision of the chief or principal investment
officers or the chief financial managers . . . ." You indicate that ACERA has a CIO
whose duties include, but are not limited to: overseeing the retirement fund's investment
program activities; and monitors the performance and compliance of ACERA's
investment managers with all applicable laws, Board-established policies, guidelines and
procedures, as well as the Specific Investment Guidelines set out in the investment
agreement between ACERA and each investment manager. According to your
description, ACERA's CIO, and maybe the Board itself as well, appear to fit the
definition of "chief or principal investment officers or chief financial managers". Since
the employees of the various investment management firms that contract with ACERA
"work under the supervision of" its CIO (and the Board, as well), they appear to be
explicitly excluded from the scope of Regulation 18701(b)(1)(B) and, therefore, also
subsection (b)(1)(C).

Therefore, based upon the facts you have provided, the employees of the various
investment management firms that contract with ACERA do not appear to fit the
definition of "other public officials who manage public investments" under Regulation
18701(b)(1) that are required to file SEI's under Section 87200 et seq.

B. "Consultants" Under Section 87300 and Regulation 18701(a)(2).

We now look at whether employees of the various investment management firms
that contract with ACERA must file SEI's as "consultants" under ACERA's conflict-of-
interest code and Section 87300.

Under the Act, "designated employees" (which constitute a subgroup of the
broader term "public official") must disclose their financial interests in accordance with
the conflict-of-interest codes developed by their agencies. (See Sections 87300 et seq.;
Section 82048 [defining "public official"]; Section 82019 [defining "designated
employee"].) The definition of a "designated employee" includes any "consultant" of
any agency whose position entails the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. (Section 82019;
see also Section 87103 [defining "financial interest"].)

A conflict-of-interest code generally requires "consultants" to disclose their
reportable investments, business positions, interests in real property, and sources of
income which may foreseeably be materially affected by their decisionmaking on behalf

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 6

of the agency. (Section 87302.) Therefore, typically, conflict-of-interest codes include
designations for consultants to the agency.

The term "consultant" is further defined in Regulation 18701(a)(2) as an
individual who, pursuant to a contract with a state or local government agency:

"(A) Makes a governmental decision whether to:
(i)
Approve a rate, rule, or regulation;
(ii)
Adopt or enforce a law;
(iii)
Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke any permit, license,
application, certificate, approval, order, or similar authorization or
entitlement;
(iv)
Authorize the agency to enter into, modify, or renew a
contract provided it is the type of contract that requires agency approval;
(v)
Grant agency approval to a contract that requires agency
approval and to which the agency is a party, or to the specifications for
such a contract;
(vi)
Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study, or
similar item;
(vii)
Adopt, or grant agency approval of, policies, standards, or
guidelines for the agency, or for any subdivision thereof; or

"(B) Serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity
participates in making a governmental decision as defined in regulation
18702.2 or performs the same or substantially all the same duties for the
agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual holding a
position specified in the agency's Conflict of Interest Code under
Government Code section 87302." (Ibid.)

Therefore, a consultant is an individual who either (A) "makes a governmental
decision" in the ways listed, or (B) "[s]erves in a staff capacity with the agency" and
either "participates in making a governmental decision" or "performs the same or
substantially all the same duties . . . that would otherwise be performed by an individual"
subject to the agency's conflict of interest code. (Ibid.; see also Wasko Advice Letter,
No. A-04-270.)

1. Regulation 18701(a)(2)(A): "Makes A Governmental Decision."

Because you state that an ACERA investment manager – that is an outside
contractor and not an employee of ACERA – has full discretion to direct the purchase,
exchange, sale and reinvestment of the assets of his or her accounts (including placing
orders to buy and sell securities), it appears that such an investment manager probably
fits the definition of Regulation 18701(a)(2)(A).

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 7

In other words, such a person most likely "[m]akes a governmental decision" to
either "[a]uthorize the agency to enter into, modify, or renew a contract provided it is the
type of contract that requires agency approval" or "[g]rant agency approval to a contract
that requires agency approval and to which the agency is a party, or to the specifications
for such a contract . . . ." (Reg. 18701(a)(2)(A), subsections (iv) and (v); see
Reg. 18702.1 [defining "makes a governmental decision"].).)

A definitive answer as to which employees of an outside investment management
firm meet this test depends upon a fact-sensitive inquiry which, in any case, requires
details regarding the tasks in which such investment managers engage.

2. Regulation 18701(a)(2)(B): "Serves In A Staff Capacity" And
"Participates In Making" or "Performs The Same Or Substantially All The
Same Duties."

Even if an individual does not make governmental decisions as described under
subsection (A) above, he or she may still be a consultant under subsection (B) if he or she
"[s]erves in a staff capacity with the agency" and either "participates in making a
governmental decision" or "performs the same or substantially all the same duties . . . that
would otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict of
interest code. (Regulation 18701(a)(2)(B).)

The phrase "serves in a staff capacity" in subsection (B) has been construed by
the Commission to include only those individuals who are performing substantially all
the same tasks that normally would be performed by one or more staff members of a
governmental entity. (Conley Advice Letter, No. A-96-192; Randolph Advice Letter,
No. I-95-045.) Therefore, persons who work on one project or a limited number of
projects for an agency over a limited period of time, would not be deemed to be serving
in a staff capacity. (Sanchez Advice Letter, No. I-03-173; Thomas Advice Letter,
No. A-98-185; Karger Advice Letter, No. A-97-253; Travis Advice Letter,
No. A-96-053; Parry Advice Letter, No. I-95-064; Randolph, supra.)

The analysis is fact-specific. We have advised that the test is not met where the
relationship between the agency and the potential consultant would last only 12-16
months and that no ongoing relationship was contemplated. (See Harris Advice Letter,
No. A-02-239.) We have also advised that a contractor working "over more than one
year" on "high-level" data processing projects meet the test. (See Ferber Advice Letter,
No. A-99-118.) We have also advised that regular and continuous work over a nine
month period in one case, did not satisfy this test. (See Smith Advice Letter,
No. I-99-316.)

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 8

If a person does not "serve in a staff capacity," they cannot be a "consultant"
under subsection (B).³ If, on the other hand, a person does serve in a staff capacity, one
must then determine whether that person also either "participates in making a
governmental decision" or "performs the same or substantially all the same duties . . . that
would otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict-of-
interest code. (Regulation 18701(a)(2)(B).) If so, they are consultants under
subsection (B).

The phrase "participates in making a governmental decision as defined in
Regulation 18702.2" refers to when a public official acts in a way that is distinct from
simply "making a governmental decision." Though the wording of the two phrases is
very similar, "making" and "participating in making" are different under the Act – the
latter phrase typically encompassing a broader sphere of activities. (Compare Reg.
18702.1 [defining "makes a governmental decision"].) A public official "participates in
making a governmental decision" by either negotiating a governmental decision (without
significant substantive review), or by advising or making recommendations to the
decisionmaker (either directly or without significant intervening substantive review).
(Regulation 18702.2.)

A public official "advises or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker" in
one of two ways: (1) conducting research or making any investigation which requires the
exercise of judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision, or (2) preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orally, or in writing which requires the exercise of judgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a governmental decision. (Reg. 18702.2(b).)

The phrase "performs the same or substantially all the same duties . . . that would
otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict of interest
code, was described in 1995 in the Randolph Advice Letter (No. I-95-045) which
interpreted the same phrase as it existed in then-Regulation 18700. (See Reg.
18701(a)(2)(B) and former Reg. 18700(a)(2)(B).)

Under the facts in the Randolph Advice Letter, Commission staff advised that
since certain employees of investment managers for the Kern County Employees'
Retirement Association ("KCERA") made decisions regarding the investment of public
funds that would otherwise have to be made by a public agency, those employees were
"consultants" under the Act and, therefore, had to disclose certain of their financial
interests under KCERA's conflict-of-interest code. Though the letter does not explicitly
indicate what time period was contemplated as the length of the investment management
contracts, some indicate "annual" performance reviews, implying they were multi-year
contracts.

³ Since we do not possess facts regarding the specific terms of any contracts between ACERA and
any of its one or more investment managers, we cannot determine whether any of ACERA's investment
managers (or their employees) "serve in a staff capacity."

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 9


The Randolph Advice Letter reasoned that investment managers were "clearly"
consultants to, at least partially, they made decisions concerning the investment of
public funds and that such decisions were ones "that would be customarily made by the
board itself or its employees." (Randolph, supra, I-95-045.) The letter states:

"Thus, in essence, the power of the board to enter into investment
contracts has been delegated to the investment managers. For these
reasons, the employees of the investment managers who provide
services to the KCERA are currently designated in the conflict of
interest code and should continue to be designated as consultants and
subject to the provisions of the Act.

"Of course, not all employees of the investment managers are considered
consultants. However, those persons with influence over the investment
decisions pertaining to the KCERA funds would appear to be
consultants . . . ." (Ibid.)

In the case of ACERA, the Board is charged with the responsibility of managing
the retirement system, and its CIO oversees the retirement fund's investment program
activities by monitoring the performance and compliance of ACERA's investment
managers with all applicable laws and policy guidelines. When it comes to specific
investment decisions, you state that, ultimately, "the ACERA investment managers have
full discretion to direct the purchase, exchange, sale and reinvestment of the assets of
their accounts," including the power to place orders to buy and sell securities. We also
note that, like in Randolph, no contract time period has been specified with regard to any
particular investment manager and assume that an on-going, multi-year relationship is
contemplated.

Thus, ACERA appears to present facts that are analogous to the facts presented in
the Randolph Advice Letter. But without: (1) information regarding the specific terms of
any contracts between ACERA and any of its one or more investment managers, or (2)
information regarding the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in
duties similar to those being performed by employees of the investment managers
described above, we cannot definitively determine whether or which investment manager
employees would fit the definition of "consultant" under the Act.

If you determine that any employees of an ACERA investment manager are
consultants under the Act, and that their making or participation in the making of
decisions may foreseeably have a material effect on any of their financial interests, then
they will have to file Statements of Economic Interests on an annual basis.

File No. I-07-076
Page No. 10


If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Andreas C. Rockas
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

AR:jgl

sections.analysis:
Under the Act, state and local "public officials" file periodic statements of
economic interests (Form 700) for the purpose of disclosing pertinent economic interests.
(See Sections 87200 et seq.; 87300 et seq.; Section 82048 [defining.,public official,,].)
These individuals are required to file the Form 700 pursuant to either Section g7200 or
Section 87300.
We will begin our analysis with Section 872}O,which imposes broad disclosure
obligations on various high level public officials, including "other public officials who
manage public investments." (Section 87200.) We will also analyze whether the people
at issue have to file SEI's as "consultants" pursuant to Section SZjOO. (Sections SZO[161
& 87300.)
A. 66Other Public Officials Who Manage Public fnvestments, Under Section g7200
and Regulation 18701(bX1).
Regulation 18701(bxl) defines "otherpublic officials who manage public
investments" to include:
"(A) Members of boards and commissions, incruding pension
and retirement boards or commissions, or of committees thereof who
exercise responsibility for the management of public investments;
"(B) High-level officers and employees ofpublic agencies who
exercise pimary responsibility for the management of public
investments, such as chief or principal investment officers or chief
financial managers. This category shall not include officers and
employees who work under the supervision of the chief or principal
investment officers or the chief financialmanagers; and
"(c) Individuals who, pursuant to a contract with a state or local
govemment agency, perform the same or substantially all the same
functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials
described in subdivision (b)(1)(B) above.,, (Ibid.)
Regulation 18701(b) (2), (3) and (4) set forth the following definitions:
"(2) 'Public investments' means the investment of public moneys
in real estate, securities, or other economic interests for the production tf
revenue or other financial return.
"(3) 'Public moneys' means all moneys belonging to, received
by, or held b5 the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public
agency therein, or by an officer thereof acting in his or her official
capacity, and includes the proceeds of all bonds and other evidences of
indebtedness, trust funds held by public pension and retirement systems,
deferred compensation funds held for investments by public agencies,
and public moneys held by a financial institution under a trustlndenture
to which a public agerrcy is a party.
. "(4) 'Management ofpublic investments' means the following
non-ministerial functions: directing the investment of public moneys;
formulating or approving investment policies; approving or establishing
guidelines for asset allocations; or approving investment transactions.,,
(rbid.)
We now analyze whether employees of the various investment management firms
that contract with ACERA are "other public officials who manage public invistments,,
under Regulation 18701(bxl). Initially, we observe that what thesi employees do

accounts . . . [and] place orders to buy and sell securities') fits the definition of

Since these employees are employed by private investment management firms,
they are neither: (A) "fm]embers of boards and commissions" 1or committees thereof;
that exercise responsibility for the management of public investments, or (B) .,[h]igh-
level officers and employees of public agencies who exercise primaryresponsiliii[, for
the management of public investments, such as chief or principal invlstrnent officers or
chief fi nan cial managers. " (See Reg. 1 8 70 I O)( 1 XA)&(B).)
In order to fit within the last category of "otherpublic officials who manage
public investments" these private employees must be "[i]ndividuals who, pursuant to a
contract with a state or local govemment agency, perform the same or substantially all
the same functions that would otherwise be performed by the public officials described in
subsection OXIXB) above." (Reg. I 3701 (bX1XC).)
Subsection (bXlXB) explicitly states "[t]his category shall not include officers
and employees who work under the supervision of the chief or principal investment
officers or the chief financialmanagers . . . ." you indicate that ACERA has a CIo
whose duties include, but are not limited to: overseeing the retirement fund,s investment
program activities; and monitors the performance and compliance of ACERA,s

definition of "chief or principal investment officers or chief financial managers,,. Since

Therefore, based upon the facts you have provided, the employees of the various
investment management firms that contract with ACERA do not appear to fit the
definition of "other public officials who manage public investmentrl' ,rrd". Regulation
18701(bX1) that are required to file SEI's under section 97200 et seq.
B. "consultants" Under section 87300 and Regulation lg70l(a)e).
We now look at whether employees of the various investment management firms
that contract with ACERA must file SEI's as "consultants" under ACERA,s conflict-of-
interest code and Section 87300.
Under the Act, "designated employees" (which constitute a subgroup of the
broader term "public official") must disclose their financial interests in accordance with
the conflict-of-interest codes developed by their agencies. (See Sections 87300 et seq.;
Section 82048 [defining "public official"]; Section 82019 [defining ..designated
employee"].) The definition of a "designated employee,, includes any,,consultant,, of
arry agerLcy whose position entails the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest. (Section 82019;
see also Section 87103 [defining "financial interest,,].)
A conflict-of-interest code generally requires "consultants" to disclose their
reportable investments, business positions, interests in real property, and sources of
income which may foreseeably be materially affected by their decisionmaking on behalf
of the agency. (Section 87302.) Therefore, typically, conflict-of-interest codes include
designations for consultants to the agency.
The term 'oconsultant" is further defined in Regulation 1g701(a)(2) as an
individual who, pursuant to a contractwith a state or local government agency:
"(A) Makes a govemmental decision whether to:
(i) 
Approve arate, rule, or regulation;
(ii) 
Adopt or enforce alaw;
(iii) 
Issue, deny, suspend, or revoke any permit, license,
application, certifrcate, approval, order, or similar authoization or
entitlement;
(iv) 
Authorize the agency to enter into, modifu, or renew a
contract provided it is the type of contract that requires agency approval;
(v) 
Grant agency approvar to a contractthatrequiies ug"""y
approval and to which the agency is a party, or to the specificatiorr fot
such a contract;
(vi) 
Grant agency approval to a plan, design, report, study, or
similar item;
(vii) 
Adopt, or grant agency approval of policies, standards, or
guidelines for the agency, or for any subdivision thereof; or
"(B) serves in a staff capacity with the agency and in that capacity
participates in making a governmental decision as defined in reguiation
18702-2 or performs the same or substantially all the same duties for the
agency that would otherwise be performed by an individual holding a
position specified in the agency's conflict of Interest code under
Government Code sectio n 87 302.,, (Ibid.)
Therefore, a consultant is an individual who either (A) "makes a govemmental
decision" in the ways listed, or (B) "[s]erves in a staff capacity with the Jgency', and
either "participates in making a govemmental decision,, or..performs theiame or
substantially all the same duties . . . that would otherwise be performed by an individual,,
subject to the agency's conflict of interest code. Qbid.; see also Wasko Advice Letter,
No. 4-04-270.)
L. Regulation 18701(aX2XA): "Makes A Governmental Decision.,,
Because you state that anACERA investment manager- that is an outside
contractor and not an employee of ACERA - has full discretion to direct the purchase,
exchange, sale and reinvestment of the assets of his or her accounts (including placing
orders to buy and sell securities), it appears that such an investment manager probably
fits the definition of Regulation 18701(a)(2XA).
In other words, such a person most likely "[m]akes a govefirmental decision,, to
either "faluthoize the agency to enter into, modifu, or renew a contract provided it is the
type of contractthatrequires ageficy approval" or "[g]rant agency approval to a contract
that requires agency approval and to which the agency is a party, or to the specifications
for such a contract . . .." (Reg. rs70l(a)Q)(A), subsections (iv) and (v); see
Reg. 18702.1 [defining "makes a govemmental decision,,].).)
A definitive answer as to which employees of an outside investment management
firm meet this test depends upon a fact-sensitive inquiry which, in any case, requires
details regarding the tasks in which such investment managers engage.
2. Regulation 18701(aX2XB): ,,Serves In A Staff Capacity,'And
'6Participates In Making" or ooPerforms The Same Or Substantialty AII The
Same Duties."
Even if an individual does not make govemmental decisions as described under
subsection (A) above, he or she may still be a consultant under subsection (B) if he or she
"[s]erves in a staff capacity with the agency''and either ,,participates in making a
governmental decision" orooperforms the same or substantially all the same duiies . . . that
would otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict of
interest code. (Regulation 1 8701 (aX2XB).)
The phrase "seryes in a staff capacitt''in subsection (B) has been construed by
the Commission to include only those individuals who are performing substantially ail
the same tasks that normally would be performed by one or more staff members of a
govemmental entity. (Conley Advice Letter, No. 4-96-192; Randolph Adviceletter,
No. I-95-045.) Therefore, persons who work on one project or a limited number of
projects for an agency over a limited period of time, would not be deemed to be serving
in a staff capacity. (Sanchez Advice Letter, No. I-03-173; Thomas Advice Letter,
No. 4-98-185; Karger Advice Letter, No. 4-97-2 53; Travis Advice Letter,
No. ,{-96-053; Parry Advice Letter,No. I-95-064; Randolph, supra.)
The analysis is fact-specific. We have advised that the test is not met where the
relationship between the agency and the potential consultant would last only 12-16
months and that no ongoing relationship was contemplated. (See Harris Advice Letter,
No. 4-02-239.) We have also advised that a contractor working "over more than one
year" on "high-level" dataprocessing projects meet the test. (See Ferber Advice Letter,
No. A-99-118.) We have also advised that regular and continuous work over a nine
month period in one case, did not satisff this test. (See Smith Advice Letter,
No. I-99-316.)
If a person does not "serve in a staff cap acity," they cannot be a "consultant,,
under subsection (B).3 Ii on the other hand, , p"r.on does serve in a staff capacity, one
must then determine whether that person also either "participates in making a
governmental decision" or "performs the same or substantially all the.u-. duti"s . . . that
would otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict-of-
interest code. (Regulation 1870L(a)Q)@).) If so, they are consultants under
subsection (B).
The phrase "participates in making a govemmental decision as defined in
Regulation 18702.2" refers to when a public official acts in away that is distinct from
simply "making a govemmental decision." Though the wording of the two phrases is
very similar, "making" and "participatrngin making" are different under the Act - the
latter phrase typically encompassing a broader sphere of activities. (Compare Reg.
18702.1[defining "makes a goveflrmental decision"].) A public official .,participates in
making a govemmental decision" by either negotiating a govefllmental decision(without
significant substantive review), or by advising or making recommendations to the
decisionmaker (either directly or without significant intervening substantive review).
(Regulation 18702.2.)
A public official "advises or makes recorlmendations to the decisionmaker,, in
one of two ways: (1) conducting research or making any investigation which requires the
exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official and the puqpose oiwhich is to influence a
govemmental decision, or (2) preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion,
orallS or in writing which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official
and the purpose of which is to influence a govemmental decision. (Reg. lg712.Z(b).)
The phrase "performs the same or substantially all the same duties . . . that would
otherwise be performed by an individual" subject to the agency's conflict of interest
code, was described in 1995 in the RandolphAdvice Letter (No. I-95-045) which
interpreted the same phrase as it existed in then-Regulation 19700. (See Ii.eg.
1 87 0 t (a)(z)(B) and former Reg. I 8 70 0(a)(2) (B ).)
Under the facts in the Randolph Advice Letter, Commission staff advised that
since certain employees of investment managers for the Kern County Employees,
Retirement Association ("KCERA") made decisions regarding the investment of public
funds that would otherwise have to be made by a public ug"r"y, those employees were
"consultants" under the Act and, therefore, had to disclose certain of their financial
interests under KCERA's conflict-of-interest code. Though the letter does not explicitly
indicate what time period was contemplated as the length of the investment management
contracts, some indicate o'anrflJal" performance reviews, implying they were *,r11i-year
contracts.
3 Since we do not possess facts regarding the specific terms of any contracts between ACERA and
any of its one or more investment managers, we cannot determine whether any of ACERA,s investment
managers (or their employees) "serve in a staff capacity.,,
The Randolph Advice Letter reasoned that investment managers were "clearly,,
consultants because, at least partially, they made decisions concerning the investment of
public funds andthat such decisions were ones "that would be customarily made by the
board itself or its employees." (Randolph, supra, r-95-045.) The letter states:
"Thus, in essence, the power of the board to enter into investment
contracts has been delegated to the investment managers. For these
reasons, the employees of the investment managers who provide
services to the KCERA are currently designated in the conflict of
interest code and should continue to be designated as consultants and
subject to the provisions of the Act.
"of course, not all employees of the investment managers are considered
consultants. However, those persons with influence over the investment
decisions pertaining to the KCERA funds would appear to be
consultants .. .." (Ibtd.)
In the case of ACERA, the Board is charged with the responsibility of managing
the retirement system, and its CIO oversees the retirement fund's investment program
activities bymonitoring the performance and compliance of ACERA,s investilent
managers with all applicable laws and policy guidelines. When it comes to specific
investment decisions, you state that, ultimately, "the ACERA investment managers have
fulI discretion to direct the purchase, exchange, sale and reinvestment of the assets of
their accounts," including the power to place orders to buy and sell securities. We also
note that, like in Randolph, no contract time period has been specified with regard to any
patticular investment marlagff and assume that an on-going, multi-year relatiolship is
contemplated.
Thus, ACERA appears to present facts that are analogous to the facts presented in
the Randolph Advice Letter. But without: (1) information regarding the rp..ifi" terms of
any contracts between ACERA and any of its one or more investment managers, or (2)
information regarding the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in
duties similar to those being performed by ernployees of the investment.urrug".,
described above, we cannot definitively determine whether or which investmeirt manager
employees would fit the definition of "consultant,, under the Act.
If you determine that any employees of an ACERA investment manager are
consultants under the Act, and that their making or participation in the making of
decisions may foreseeably have a material effect on any of their financial intJests, then
they will have to file Statements of Economic Interests on an annual basis.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are employees of the various investment management firms that contract with the
Alameda County Employees' Retirement Association ("ACERA") included within the
definition of "consultant" under the Act such that they are required to file Statements of
Economic Interests ("SEI" or "Form 700,,)?
I Government Code sections 8 I 000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2,sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All references io code "sdc-tions,, shall be to the
Government Code, and all references to "regulations" shall be to title 2 of the state,s Code of Regulations,
unless otherwise noted.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Reg. 18329(c).)
:--;

CONCLUSION: It appears that at least some employees of investment managers hired by ACERA,
possess a level of discretion and influence over investment decisions regardingACERA,s
funds that would make them subject to economic disclosure provisions underihe Act.
But without: (1) information regarding the specific terms of any contracts between
ACERA and arry of its one or more investment managers, or (2) information regarding
the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in duties similar to those
being performed by employees of the investment managers described above, we cannot
definitively determine whether or which investment manager employees would be
"consultants" under the Act, and obligated to file Statements of Economic Interests.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 June 20, 2007 Robert Gaumer Chief Counsel Alameda County Employees' Retirement Assoc. 475 14th Street, Suite 1000 Oakland, California 94612 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-07-076 Dear Mr. Gaumer: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding certain financial interest disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflicts of interest or Government Code Section 1090. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when providing assistance; this assistance is based solely on the facts you provide. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Thus, we are providing you with general advice and are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² QUESTION Are employees of the various investment management firms that contract with the Alameda County Employees' Retirement Association ("ACERA") included within the definition of "consultant" under the Act such that they are required to file Statements of Economic Interests ("SEI" or "Form 700")? _______________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All references to code "sections" shall be to the Government Code, and all references to "regulations" shall be to title 2 of the state's Code of Regulations, unless otherwise noted. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Reg. 18329(c).) File No. I-07-076 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION It appears that at least some employees of investment managers hired by ACERA, possess a level of discretion and influence over investment decisions regarding ACERA's funds that would make them subject to economic disclosure provisions under the Act. But without: (1) information regarding the specific terms of any contracts between ACERA and any of its one or more investment managers, or (2) information regarding the extent to which designated employees of ACERA engage in duties similar to those being performed by employees of the investment managers described above, we cannot definitively determine whether or which investment manager employees would be "consultants" under the Act, and obligated to file Statements of Economic Interests. FACTS The Alameda County Employees' Retirement Association ("ACERA") is an independent public pension fund established under the authority of the County Employees Retirement Law of 1937 (Sections 31450 et seq.). ACERA's Board of Retirement ("Board") is comprised of nine members and two alternates. The Board is charged with the responsibility of managing the retirement system, including investment of the retirement fund. The Board has the sole authority to develop and execute the investment policy. The investments are based on a comprehensive financial plan that takes into consideration various investment mixes, their resulting risk and return levels, and the current and projected
